mnemonics.title: C.H.U.R.C.H.
mnemonics.content: C – Computable, H – Hypothesis, U – Unambiguous, R – Recursive, C – Church, H – Turing. The phrase Computable Hypothesis Underlies Recursive Church–Turing reminds you that the thesis equates computability across models (λ‑calc, Turing machines, recursive functions).

mnemonics.title: TLR = All the Same
mnemonics.content: T (Turing Machines) = L (Lambda Calculus) = R (Recursive Functions). Think of the three as different languages saying the same thing: any function that one can compute, all can compute.

mnemonics.title: Universal Computer Picture
mnemonics.content: Imagine a tape (Turing) that can be rewound, a function (λ) that can be substituted, and a recursion (primitive/μ‑recursive) that loops; all three converge to a single universal computer icon in the center of your mind.

acronyms.usage: Used when discussing the canonical model of computation; any algorithm can be expressed as a TM program.
A formal system for function definition and application; equivalent in power to TM.
A class of total computable functions; together with μ‑operator yields general recursion (μ‑recursive functions).
The most general class of computable functions; includes unbounded search (μ operator).

visualTips.visualization: Picture an infinite tape divided into cells, a read‑write head moving left/right, and a finite control table that decides the next state and symbol – think of it as a mechanical computer.
Imagine a sheet of paper where you write a function f, then apply an argument x by crossing out f and writing the body of f with x substituted – like a recipe step.
Visualize a loop icon that repeats a base case until a termination condition is met; the μ‑operator appears as a magnifying glass searching for a value that makes the predicate true.

analogies.analogy: It's like different spoken languages (English, Hindi, French) all can express the same ideas; no language can convey something that another cannot.
Think of a set of building blocks (LEGO). Turing machines, lambda calculus, and recursive functions are just different coloured blocks that can build the same structures.
Like a physical law (e.g., conservation of energy) – it's a principle that all realistic computers obey, even though it's a thesis, not a theorem.
